---
title: "R Episode 10 — Functions Explained"
---

::: {.callout-note}
## Objectives
By the end of this episode you will be able to:

- Define functions with arguments, defaults, and return values.
- Understand scope (what a function can and can't see).
- Document a function so others (and future-you) can use it.

**Teaching:** 35 min · **Exercises:** 25 min
:::

## Defining a function

```r
fahr_to_celsius <- function(temp) {
  (temp - 32) * 5 / 9
}

fahr_to_celsius(32)     # 0
fahr_to_celsius(212)    # 100
```

The last evaluated expression is returned automatically; use `return()` for early exits.

## Arguments and defaults

```r
gdp_total <- function(gapminder, year = NULL) {
  data <- gapminder
  if (!is.null(year)) {
    data <- data[data$year == year, ]
  }
  data$pop * data$gdpPercap
}
```

Defaults make functions flexible: callers can rely on sensible behavior or override it.

## Scope

Variables created inside a function are local — they don't leak out, and the function can't see
your console variables unless you pass them in:

```r
f <- function() { y <- 10; y }
f()        # 10
# y        # Error: object 'y' not found  (y is local to f)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Instructor note
Emphasize "pass data in, return results out" — never rely on global state. This is the habit that
makes functions testable (Episode 15) and reusable. Show how a function that reads a global
variable breaks the moment that variable changes.
:::

## Documenting functions

Write a short comment describing purpose, parameters, and return value. For packages, the
**roxygen2** style turns these into real help pages:

```r
#' Convert Fahrenheit to Celsius
#'
#' @param temp Numeric vector of temperatures in Fahrenheit.
#' @return Numeric vector of temperatures in Celsius.
fahr_to_celsius <- function(temp) {
  (temp - 32) * 5 / 9
}
```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Challenge — Compose functions
Write `celsius_to_kelvin()` (add 273.15), then a `fahr_to_kelvin()` that *reuses*
`fahr_to_celsius()` and `celsius_to_kelvin()`.

### Solution

```r
celsius_to_kelvin <- function(temp) temp + 273.15
fahr_to_kelvin <- function(temp) celsius_to_kelvin(fahr_to_celsius(temp))
fahr_to_kelvin(212)   # 373.15
```
Composing small functions beats one big one — each piece is testable.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Key points
- `function(args) { body }`; the last expression is returned.
- Defaults make functions flexible; pass data in, return results out.
- Function scope is local — avoid relying on globals.
- Document purpose, `@param`, and `@return`; compose small functions.
:::

Next: [Episode 11 — Reading & Writing Data](r-11-reading-writing-data.qmd).
